content.problemStatement:
Roger recently built a circular race track with length KKK. After hosting a few races, he realised that people do not come there to see the race itself, they come to see racers crash into each other (what's wrong with our generation...). After this realisation, Roger decided to hold a different kind of "races": he hired NNN racers (numbered 111 through NNN) whose task is to crash into each other.

At the beginning, for each valid iii, the iii-th racer is XiX_iXi​ metres away from the starting point of the track (measured in the clockwise direction) and driving in the direction DiD_iDi​ (clockwise or counterclockwise). All racers move with the constant speed 111 metre per second. The lengths of cars are negligible, but the track is only wide enough for one car, so whenever two cars have the same position along the track, they crash into each other and the direction of movement of each of these cars changes (from clockwise to counterclockwise and vice versa). The cars do not change the directions of their movement otherwise.

Since crashes reduce the lifetime of the racing cars, Roger sometimes wonders how many crashes happen. You should answer QQQ queries. In each query, you are given an integer TTT and you should find the number of crashes that happen until TTT seconds have passed (inclusive).
constraints: X1, X2, ..., XN are pairwise distinct
content.inputFormat: First line contains three space-separated integers N, Q, and K. N lines follow. Each line contains two space-separated integers Di and Xi. Each of the next Q lines contains a single integer T.
content.outputFormat: For each query, print a single line containing one integer — the number of crashes.

solution.approach: We will use a sweep line approach to solve this problem. We will first sort the racers by their initial positions. Then, we will iterate over the racers and update the number of crashes at each position. We will use a segment tree to store the number of crashes at each position.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N + Q log K) with N being the number of racers and Q being the number of queries.
